---
title: "Cross-Entropy"
subtitle: "Quantifying the inefficiency of using a wrong assumption to predict a true distribution."
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
---

## 1. Definitions {#sec-definitions}

::: {.thm-box .thm-definition}
### Definition: Cross-Entropy

For discrete distributions $P$ and $Q$ defined over the same sample space $\mathcal{X}$, the cross-entropy $H(P, Q)$ is defined as the expected surprisal of the estimated distribution $Q$, evaluated under the true distribution $P$:

$$
H(P, Q) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P} [ -\log_2 Q(x) ] = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 Q(x)
$$

For continuous distributions with probability density functions $p$ and $q$, the sum is replaced by an integral:
$$
H(P, Q) = -\int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln q(x) \, dx
$$
:::

---

## 2. Intuitions & Remarks {#sec-intuitions}

::: {.thm-box .thm-remark}
### The Cost of Being Wrong

In information theory, **Cross-Entropy** measures the average number of bits needed to identify an event drawn from a true probability distribution $P$, if a coding scheme is used that is optimized for an estimated (or unnatural) probability distribution $Q$, rather than the true distribution $P$.

Intuitively, if you believe the weather follows distribution $Q$ (e.g., mostly sunny), you will design a communication protocol that assigns very short codes to the message "sunny". If the *actual* weather follows distribution $P$ (e.g., mostly rainy), your protocol will be highly inefficient because you will frequently have to transmit the long, complex codes for "rainy". 

Cross-entropy quantifies this exact inefficiency. It asks: "How many bits on average do I have to transmit if I optimize for $Q$, but reality actually follows $P$?"
:::

---

## 3. Formulas & Key Properties {#sec-properties}

::: {.thm-box .thm-theorem}
### Key Properties of Cross-Entropy

#### 1. Non-Symmetric
Cross-entropy is asymmetric. The cost of assuming $Q$ when reality is $P$ is completely different from assuming $P$ when reality is $Q$.
$$
H(P, Q) \neq H(Q, P)
$$

#### 2. Lower Bound (Gibbs' Inequality)
Cross-entropy is strictly bounded below by the true entropy of the ground truth distribution $P$.
$$
H(P, Q) \ge H(P)
$$
Equality holds if and only if your estimation perfectly matches reality ($P = Q$).

#### 3. Relationship to KL Divergence
Cross-entropy is deeply connected to Shannon Entropy and the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence via a profound decomposition:
$$
H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)
$$
This equation states that the total cost of using the wrong distribution $Q$ is exactly equal to the irreducible, theoretical minimum cost if you knew the true distribution $P$ (which is $H(P)$), plus a "penalty gap" for your estimation error (which is the KL Divergence).
:::

---

## 4. Graphs & Visualizations {#sec-visualizations}

The relationship expressed by $H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)$ can be visualized through a simple decomposition of the penalty.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    P[True Distribution P\n(Reality)] -->|Irreducible Uncertainty| H_P(Entropy H)
    Q[Estimated Distribution Q\n(Assumption)] -.->|Estimation Error| KL(KL Divergence Penalty)
    P -.->|Compared against| KL
    
    H_P --> CE{Cross-Entropy H P,Q}
    KL --> CE
    
    style P fill:#161b22,stroke:#00ff88,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#161b22,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H_P fill:#161b22,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style KL fill:#161b22,stroke:#8b5cf6,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style CE fill:#161b22,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

## 5. Relations to Other Fields {#sec-relations}

| Field | Interpretation / Mapping | Application Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Deep Learning** | The most universally used loss function for classification tasks. | Training neural networks |
| **Information Theory** | The length of messages encoded with a suboptimal codebook. | Kraft-McMillan inequality |

::: {.thm-box .thm-example}
### Machine Learning Context: Cross-Entropy Loss

When training a neural network classifier, we are trying to predict the true label distribution $P$ using the model's predicted distribution $Q$. 
- The true distribution $P$ for a single sample is typically a **one-hot encoded vector** (e.g., $[0, 1, 0, 0]$ where the target class has probability 1).
- The predicted distribution $Q$ is the output of the **Softmax function** (e.g., $[0.1, 0.7, 0.15, 0.05]$).

If we minimize the Cross-Entropy $H(P, Q)$, we push $Q$ to be as close to $P$ as possible. 

#### Why is $H(P)$ ignored in practice?
Recall the decomposition $H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)$. 
In a classification task, the true labels $P$ are deterministic (one-hot), meaning the true entropy $H(P)$ is exactly $0$. Therefore, minimizing Cross-Entropy is mathematically identical to minimizing the KL Divergence between the predictions and the ground truth!
:::

---

## 6. Interactive Simulation {#sec-simulation}

```{=html}
<div class="simulation-card" style="background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.03); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); border-radius: 16px; padding: 2rem; margin: 2rem 0; box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(0,0,0,0.4); backdrop-filter: blur(12px);">
  <h4 style="margin-top: 0; color: #fff; font-family: 'Outfit', sans-serif;">Interactive Simulation: Binary Cross-Entropy Loss</h4>
  <p style="color: #a0a2b6; font-size: 0.95rem; margin-bottom: 1.5rem;">
    Assume the true ground-truth label is $y=1$ (deterministic, $H(P)=0$). Drag the slider to change the model's predicted probability $q$. Watch how the Cross-Entropy loss explodes to infinity as the model becomes highly confident but totally wrong (predicting $q \approx 0$).
  </p>

  <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 1.5rem; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto;">
    <!-- SVG Chart -->
    <svg id="ce-chart" viewBox="0 0 500 250" style="width: 100%; height: auto; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.2); border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.05);">
      <!-- Grid lines -->
      <line x1="50" y1="200" x2="450" y2="200" stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)" stroke-width="1" />
      <line x1="50" y1="30" x2="50" y2="200" stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)" stroke-width="1" />

      <!-- Axis Labels -->
      <text x="250" y="230" fill="#8b949e" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Outfit">Predicted Probability q</text>
      <text x="20" y="115" fill="#8b949e" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 20 115)" font-family="Outfit">Cross-Entropy Loss</text>

      <text x="50" y="215" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">0.0</text>
      <text x="250" y="215" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">0.5</text>
      <text x="450" y="215" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">1.0</text>

      <text x="40" y="203" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="end">0</text>
      <text x="40" y="115" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="end">3</text>
      <text x="40" y="34" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="end">6</text>

      <!-- Curve path -->
      <path id="ce-curve" d="" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.8" />

      <!-- Current selection markers -->
      <line id="ce-h-guide" x1="50" y1="0" x2="0" y2="0" stroke="rgba(245, 158, 11, 0.3)" stroke-dasharray="4,4" />
      <line id="ce-v-guide" x1="0" y1="200" x2="0" y2="0" stroke="rgba(245, 158, 11, 0.3)" stroke-dasharray="4,4" />
      <circle id="ce-point" cx="0" cy="0" r="6" fill="#f59e0b" filter="drop-shadow(0 0 4px #f59e0b)" />
    </svg>

    <!-- Controls and Readout -->
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto; gap: 1.5rem; align-items: center; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.15); padding: 1rem; border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.03);">
      <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0.5rem;">
        <label for="ce-slider" style="color: #c9d1d9; font-size: 0.85rem; font-weight: 600; font-family: 'Outfit';">Set Predicted Prob q:</label>
        <input id="ce-slider" type="range" min="0.01" max="0.99" step="0.01" value="0.5" style="width: 100%; accent-color: #f59e0b; cursor: pointer;" />
      </div>
      <div style="text-align: right; font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.9rem; line-height: 1.4; color: #fff;">
        <div>q = <span id="ce-val-q" style="color: #60a5fa; font-weight: 700;">0.500</span></div>
        <div>Loss = <span id="ce-val-loss" style="color: #f59e0b; font-weight: 700;">1.000</span> nats</div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <script>
    document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function() {
      const slider = document.getElementById("ce-slider");
      const valQ = document.getElementById("ce-val-q");
      const valLoss = document.getElementById("ce-val-loss");
      const path = document.getElementById("ce-curve");
      const point = document.getElementById("ce-point");
      const hGuide = document.getElementById("ce-h-guide");
      const vGuide = document.getElementById("ce-v-guide");

      const width = 400; // 450 - 50
      const height = 170; // 200 - 30

      // BCE with true y=1, so Loss = -ln(q)
      function CE(q) {
          if (q <= 0) return 10; // Cap for visualization
          return -Math.log(q); // Use nats for ML contexts
      }

      // Generate SVG path for the curve
      let d = "";
      for (let q = 0.005; q <= 1.0; q += 0.005) {
        const x = 50 + q * width;
        const h = CE(q);
        const y = 200 - (h / 6.0) * height; // Scale up to 6 nats
        d += (q === 0.005 ? "M" : "L") + `${x} ${y}`;
      }
      path.setAttribute("d", d);

      function update() {
        const q = parseFloat(slider.value);
        const h = CE(q);

        valQ.textContent = q.toFixed(3);
        valLoss.textContent = h.toFixed(3);

        const x = 50 + q * width;
        const y = 200 - (h / 6.0) * height;

        point.setAttribute("cx", x);
        point.setAttribute("cy", y);

        hGuide.setAttribute("x1", 50);
        hGuide.setAttribute("y1", y);
        hGuide.setAttribute("x2", x);
        hGuide.setAttribute("y2", y);

        vGuide.setAttribute("x1", x);
        vGuide.setAttribute("y1", 200);
        vGuide.setAttribute("x2", x);
        vGuide.setAttribute("y2", y);
      }

      slider.addEventListener("input", update);
      update();
    });
  </script>
</div>
```

---

## 7. Practice Problems {#sec-problems}

### Problem 1: Decomposition of Cross-Entropy {#problem-1 .thm-box .thm-problem}

Mathematically derive the decomposition $H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)$ from the base definition of Cross-Entropy. *(Hint: Use the definition of KL Divergence $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$)*.
<details class="solution-toggle">
<summary>Reveal Solution to Problem 1</summary>
<div class="solution-body">

::: {.thm-box .thm-solution}
<div class="solution-heading">Solution</div>

Starting from the definition of KL Divergence:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(P \| Q) &= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right) \\
&= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \big( \log P(x) - \log Q(x) \big) \\
&= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log P(x) - \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log Q(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice that the first term is exactly the negative entropy of $P$, and the second term is the positive Cross-Entropy $H(P, Q)$:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -H(P) + H(P, Q)
$$

Rearranging the terms, we get the fundamental relationship:
$$
H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)
$$

$\blacksquare$
:::

</div>
</details>

### Problem 2: Binary Cross-Entropy Penalty Explosion {#problem-2 .thm-box .thm-problem}

For a binary classification task, the Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) formula simplifies to:
$$
\text{BCE} = - \big( y \ln(p) + (1 - y) \ln(1 - p) \big)
$$
Calculate the BCE loss for a single data point where the true label is $y=1$ and the model's predicted probability is $p=0.001$. Why does the loss become so massive compared to a wrong but less confident prediction (e.g. $p=0.4$)?
<details class="solution-toggle">
<summary>Reveal Solution to Problem 2</summary>
<div class="solution-body">

::: {.thm-box .thm-solution}
<div class="solution-heading">Solution</div>

If $y = 1$, the second term $(1 - y)$ becomes zero, completely eliminating the $\ln(1-p)$ term. The formula simplifies to:
$$
\text{BCE} = -\ln(p)
$$

**For $p = 0.4$ (Wrong, but not confidently wrong):**
$$
\text{BCE} = -\ln(0.4) \approx 0.916 \text{ nats}
$$

**For $p = 0.001$ (Highly confident, but completely wrong):**
$$
\text{BCE} = -\ln(0.001) \approx 6.907 \text{ nats}
$$

The loss is over 7 times higher! The logarithmic nature of the loss function massively penalizes a model that is *confidently wrong*. As the probability $p$ approaches exactly $0$ for a true class, the loss approaches positive infinity ($\lim_{p \to 0} -\ln(p) = \infty$). This asymmetric explosion forces the model to heavily adjust its weights to never guarantee an incorrect prediction.
:::

</div>
</details>
